coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots doped with nitrogen for rapid detection of Fe (III) and preparation of fluorescent films for optoelectronic devices
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bing
Luo, Yixin
Peng, Binqiong
Zhang, Lisha
Xie, Ning
Yue, Dan
Li, Weidong
Qin, Bowen
Du, Wenjing
Wang, Zhenling
Zhang, Yilei
coredata.dc:description: The paper presents a green and easily prepared water-soluble fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) synthesized by one-step hydrothermal process. The NCDs displays brilliant blue fluorescence excited by UV light, with excellent water solubility, and a quantum yield of 15.7 %. The fluorescence intensity of NCDs remains unchanged after 48 h of exposure to ultraviolet light, exhibiting good photostability. Moreover, the ion sensing characteristics of these NCDs have been explored for more than a dozen anions and cations. The result reflects that this fluorescent CDs exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity for Fe3+ within a range spanning of 40 ∼ 500 μΜ with a limit of detection down to 0.22 μΜ. The principle of sensor sensing is likely related to the formation of the internal filtering effect (IFE), which leads to a decline in the fluorescence intensity of NCDs in aqueous solutions containing Fe3+. Due to the excellent water solubility, good photostability and high yield of NCDs, the application of prepared NCDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as fluorescent materials for white LED devices was explored. The prepared NCDs/PVA (NCP) composite film can also exhibit bright blue light under 365 nm excitation, indicating good application prospects in the area of white LED devices and agricultural films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Metallic ion detection
N-CDs/PVA (NCP) film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NCDs and NCP film
The components and structure of NCDs and NCP film
Ion selectivity and sensitivity of N-doped CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images (a) and the size distribution histogram (b) of the NCDs; and SEM micrograph (c) of NCP film.
FTIR spectra of PVA, NCDs and NCP films.
(a) Full survey XPS spectrum of NCDs; the high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s in NCDs.
The powder XRD patterns of NCP films with different NCDs contents.

The FL excitation and emission spectra (inset: photos taken under visible light (left) and ultraviolet light (right)) of NCDs aqueous solution (a); FL emission spectra of NCDs aqueous solution under the excitation of different wavelengths (b) and at different pH values excited by 320 nm (c); and FL emission spectra with different UV (365 nm) exposure times (d).
FL intensity ratios (I/I0) of NCDs solution with and without various metallic ions (300 μM). The error bars represent the standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

(a) FL emission spectra of NCDs with various concentrations of Fe (Ⅲ); (b) the relationship curve between the logarithm of Fe(Ⅲ) concentration and (F0-F)/F0, the inset at the top-left corner represents the calibration curve.
Luminescence decay curves of NCDs with and without Fe(Ⅲ) at 430 nm emission.
(a) Effect of pH on fluorescence quenching efficiency; (b) Effect of reaction time on fluorescence quenching efficiency.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NCDs (a); and UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of PVA films with different concentration of NCDs (b).

(a) The FL spectra of NCP film; (b) the emission spectra of NCP films with different content of NCDs excited by 320 nm; (c) FL spectra of NCP films excited by different wavelengths; (d) the photograph of NCP films taken under both natural light and 365 nm UV light.
Flowchart for preparation of NCDs and NCP film and determination of Fe (Ⅲ) using NCDs.
Comparison of Fe(III) determination methodologies utilizing carbon dot nanomaterials in recent years.
The practical identification of Fe (Ⅲ) in tap water.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots doped with nitrogen for rapid detection of Fe (III) and preparation of fluorescent films for optoelectronic devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Civil & Mechanical Engineering University of Canterbury, Christchurch 8140, New Zealand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Civil & Mechanical Engineering University of Canterbury

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dCivil & Mechanical Engineering University of Canterbury, Christchurch 8140, New Zealand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paper presents a green and easily prepared water-soluble fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) synthesized by one-step hydrothermal process. The NCDs displays brilliant blue fluorescence excited by UV light, with excellent water solubility, and a quantum yield of 15.7 %. The fluorescence intensity of NCDs remains unchanged after 48 h of exposure to ultraviolet light, exhibiting good photostability. Moreover, the ion sensing characteristics of these NCDs have been explored for more than a dozen anions and cations. The result reflects that this fluorescent CDs exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity for Fe3+ within a range spanning of 40 ∼ 500 μΜ with a limit of detection down to 0.22 μΜ. The principle of sensor sensing is likely related to the formation of the internal filtering effect (IFE), which leads to a decline in the fluorescence intensity of NCDs in aqueous solutions containing Fe3+. Due to the excellent water solubility, good photostability and high yield of NCDs, the application of prepared NCDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as fluorescent materials for white LED devices was explored. The prepared NCDs/PVA (NCP) composite film can also exhibit bright blue light under 365 nm excitation, indicating good application prospects in the area of white LED devices and agricultural films.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent years have witnessed rapid development of photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs) because of their low cost, environmental-friendly, good biocompatibility, versatility, tunable and stable fluorescence properties. Their applications in various fields have been explored, including biolabeling [1,2], bioimaging [3–5], sensing [6–8], electrocatalysis [9,10], optoelectronic devices [11–14], and so on. According to previous reports, the photoluminescence mechanism of CDs is mainly attributed to quantum size effect, surface state, molecular state, crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) and environmental effect [8,15-17]. Various strategies based on above mechanisms have been proposed to modify the structure and regulate the luminescent properties of CDs [17]. Among them, heteroatoms doping of CDs has become a mainstream strategy to effectively improve their optical property by regulating the surface state of CDs [18–22]. Doping with heteroatoms is commonly adopted to regulate the optical properties of CDs. Yin et al. [23] synthesized a N/S co-doped CDs with significantly enhanced photoluminescence, employing tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) as the sole precursor for carbon and nitrogen and sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8) as the precursor for sulfur. Atchudan and co-authors [24] synthesized hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots characterized by robust and adjustable fluorescence (FL) emission, along with a commendable quantum yield of 23 %. Wu et al. [25] employed a hydrothermal approach to fabricate nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), utilizing microcrystalline cellulose as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen dopant. In comparison to the undoped CDs, its fluorescence emission intensity is enhanced by 7.1 times. Doping with N and S can significantly improve the luminous efficiency or bring about changes in fluorescence characteristics of CDs, making them the most studied heteroatoms introduced in carbon dots. However, the exact mechanism by which heteroatoms doping improves the luminescent performance of CDs is still controversial and needs further exploration. Citric acid is a commonly used carbon source for carbon dots, and polyamine materials are often used as nitrogen sources, which could improve the luminescence efficiency and make the emission spectra redshift of pure carbon dots. Normally, incorporating a carbon core can impart distinctive properties, such as spectral redshift and excitation-independence, to carbon dots. This arises from the interaction between the non-paired electrons from oxygen or nitrogen atoms of surface groups and the conjugated π . electrons thin the carbon core. Chen et al. [26] selected p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) as the precursor to prepare a novel N-doped CDs which demonstrate intense red emission independent of excitation wavelength. Yan and co-workers [14] proposed a single-step solvothermal strategy for the production of white-light emission carbon dots (WCDs) employing o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and trimellitic acid as the reactants with excitation-independent characteristics. As an important central nervous system neurotransmitter, dopamine is also a polyamine material and contains a rigid benzene ring, which could facilitate the formation of rigid carbon core. Therefore, using dopamine as a nitrogen source material is expected to yield carbon dots with excellent luminescent properties. However, there are currently few reports on using dopamine as a reactant to prepare nitrogen doped carbon dots.

Besides, although the preparation of CDs is simpler and faster, some CDs solutions require low-temperature storage, and long-term storage can cause aggregation and precipitation, resulting in decreased fluorescence intensity. By preparing CDs into solid materials, such as paper-based sensors and membrane sensors, fluorescence quenching caused by π - π accumulation in CDs can be suppressed [27–31]. Moreover, the solid phase materials are stable and easy-store, avoiding the use of additional solvents and reagents, greatly improving the practical applicability, which is conducive to the development of portable detection equipment and the use of non-professional detection personnel. Thus, CDs@polymer composite films have also attracted widespread research interest in solid-state lighting, and other fields [32]. CDs@polymer fluorescent films are usually prepared by mixing CDs with polymers into a solution and evaporating the solvent [33,34]. In CDs@polymer fluorescent films, the presence of hydrogen bonding can enhance the binding force between CDs and polymers, promote the dispersion of CDs, and improve the performance of fluorescent films at last. The polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymers contain plenty of values of hydroxyl groups as effective corresponding functional groups for interactions with -COOH, -NH2, and -OH groups on the CDs surface. The organized network structure generated by the incorporation of CDs and PVA matrix could limit the vibration motion of the PVA chain and therefore, enhance the fluorescence stability of the film. [35].

Here we adopted citric acid and dopamine hydrochloride as raw materials to prepare a novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs). The as-synthesized sample exhibits vibrant blue fluorescence, characterized by a fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of 15.7 %. Further research results show that such NCDs solution represents good selectivity in the determination of Fe3+, which reflect its enormous potential in sensing and biomedical testing applications. The mechanism of the sensor for Fe3+ detection has been discussed in depth in the paper. Moreover, a flat and smooth composite membrane with high excitation-independent blue fluorescent has been prepared by mixing NCDs into PVA solution, which offered particular hope for the application of the NCDs in LED devices or other optical conversion fields.

Citric acid and dopamine hydrochloride were acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dodecyl sodium sulfonate (C12H25SO3Na) and glycerol (C3H8O3) were purchased from Tianjin No. 3 Chemical Plant Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). PVA were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The metal ion solutions used were all made by their nitrates and the anionic solutions used were all made by their sodium salts. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCDs and NCP film

ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 10 mmol of citric acid and 10 mmol of dopamine hydrochloride were dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water at room temperature, and the pH was adjusted to 6 with sodium hydroxide. The solution was then transferred to a 150 mL hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 4 h. Following natural cooling to room temperature, the resulting product underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, followed by filtration through a membrane filter (0.22 μm). Finally, the solution was dialyzed with membrane (1000 Da) for 24 h to remove salts and acids. The CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying the purified solution which has been concentrated using a rotary evaporator.

6 g PVA was firstly dissolved in 31 mL ultra-pure water by magnetically agitated at 90 °C for 3 h. After PVA is completely dissolved, add 0.5 g sodium dodecyl sulfate and 2.5 g glycerol, continue to heat and stir at 90 °C for 1 h, which is used to help demould PVA films. Subsequently, add a certain amount of CDs powder (0.2 g, 0.4 g, 0.6 g, 0.8 g) and continue to stir for 0.5 h, forming the homogenous gelatinous liquid. And then taking a proper amount of solution to the clean glass plate placed horizontally, and scraping it to be flat with SZQ wet film preparator (thickness about 150 μm). After dried in a furnace at 80 °C for 1 hour, the uniform and smooth NCP films were obtained. Finally, the fluorescence composite films were cut into several 20 × 20 mm sheet shaped square for subsequent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The components and structure of NCDs and NCP film
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology and crystal structure of the NCDs were examined using TEM and HR-TEM. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) reveals that the as-prepared NCDs show ∼5 nm in diameter with a spheroidal shape. As shown in the HRTEM image, the interplanar spacing of the nanoparticles is approximately 0.21 nm, which is typical lattice plane of graphitic carbon [36]. The images of the composite films were measured by SEM. The as-prepared film is flat and uniform, making it easy for the production and practical use in solid-state fluorescence devices (Fig. 1c). Although the color of the film turns brown with the raising of carbon quantum dots, the microscopic appearance of the films has not been greatly affected because the average diameter of CDs is usually less than 10 nm, which is too small to be observed [37]. However, with excessive carbon quantum dots, holes and wrinkles in the composite film begin to appear (Fig. S1), which may be attributed to the connection and flaking of NCDs.

The functional groups present in the NCDs, pure PVA and NCP films are further explored by FT-IR measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 , NCDs has strong characteristic absorption peaks of the free and associated -NH2 groups at around 3410 and 3125 cm−1. The absorptive peak exhibited at approximately 1624 cm−1 can be ascribed to the bending vibrations of NH bonds, while the peaks detected at 1460 and 1398 cm−1 can be ascribed to the bending vibrations of CH bonds. As for PVA, the strong absorption peak located at 3245 cm−1 corresponds to -OH group stretching vibration. The crest peaks that appeared at 1410 and 1320 cm−1 are associated with the saturated CH bond out-of-plane bending vibration. The CO bond stretching vibration is occurred at around 1094 and 1030 cm−1. The -CH2- swaying vibration in the PVA is around 842 cm−1. Upon compounding NCDs with PVA film, the new absorption peaks near 1580 and 1650cm−1 emerge as a result of the existence of NH and C = N functional groups, respectively. [7] This phenomenon verifies the successful recombination of NCDs with PVA.

To further confirm the surface composition and chemical valence state of the NCDs, XPS characterization was conducted. Fig. 3 presents the XPS full-scan spectra, along with the corresponding high-resolution spectra for C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s of the synthesized NCDs powders. The three quintessential peaks at the binding energy of 284.3, 399.7 and 532.7 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s respectively, which confirms the existence of elements of C, N, and O on the surface of NCDs. The spectrum of the C1s region is divided into three Gaussian peaks (Fig. 3b), located at approximately 284.4, 285.1, and 286.4 eV, belonging separately to C=C/CC, CN-C/CO-C, and C = O/C = N bonds. [38,39]. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum shown in Fig. 3c can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 398.92 and 400.5 eV, indicating that the N in NCDs mainly exists in the forms of pyridinic CN-C and graphitic (C)3-N [25,40]. The O 1 s spectrum of NCDs is converted into three Gaussian peaks centered at 531.2, 532.3, and 533.8 eV. These peaks are assigned to C–OH/CO-C, C = O, and CO functionalities, respectively [32]. The good consistency between XPS results and FTIR analysis results further demonstrates the effective incorporation of nitrogen into the NCDs structure.

Next, the phase structures of CDs, PVA and NCP film were analyzed using powder XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. 4 , there is only one broadband located at 2θ =21.5° in XRD pattern of CDs, corresponding to the graphite [002] plane. The main diffraction peak in the XRD patterns of the pure PVA film was at 2θ =19.6°. With the doping of NCDs, the diffraction peak position of PVA remains unchanged, while the intensity of the diffraction peaks at 21.5° gradually become stronger, indicating an increase in the doping amount of NCDs. This is due to the presence of a large number of functional groups (namely NH2) in NCDs that can interact with hydroxyl groups in PVA. Owing to the influence of these micro-cross-linking and other effects, PVA molecules become more difficult to penetrate into the lattice, leading to a decrease in the crystallinity of the composite membrane. From the above results, it is further substantiated that the carbon dots have been well integrated into the PVA film.

The FL performance of NCDs was subsequently investigated in detail using fluorescence spectroscopy. N-doped CDs were dispersed in aqueous solution at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1. As described in Fig. 5 a, the NCDs solution possesses a wide excitation band from 275 nm to 400 nm. When exposed to ultraviolet light (365 nm), the light-brown NCDs solution exhibits bright blue fluorescence. Fig. 5b presents that the NCDs has typical excitation-dependent fluorescence performance. When the incident light wavelength increases from 280 to 340 nm at intervals of 10 nm, the emission band of the solution undergoes a red shift, which might be ascribed to the heterogeneity on the surface of NCDs. [15] The NCDs exhibits the maximum emission wavelength at about 430 nm excited by 320 nm. The influence of pH on fluorescence performance was then investigated by changing pH values from 1 to 14. It can be observed from Fig. 5c, the maximum emission wavelength remains almost unchanged under other pH conditions, while undergoes a shift towards longer wavelengths, transitions from 430 nm to 445 nm at pH = 1, and to 438 nm at pH = 14, exhibiting good stability of NCDs whithin a broad pH range varying from 3 to 11. The reduction in fluorescence intensity and the shift in emission peak positions at low and high pH values may be ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of acidic and amine groups on the NCDs surface. [41] Then, the photostability of NCDs was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, even after continuous exposure to UV light (365 nm) for 48 h, no noticeable alterations were observed in the emission spectra of the NCDs, which indicated the NCDs solution has excellent photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ion selectivity and sensitivity of N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots have sparked significant interest in their sensing capabilities due to their excellent photoluminescence properties, easy surface functionalization, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. It is well known that the FL performance of CDs is often affected by the interplay between the functional groups possessing multiple coordination sites attached to the surfaces of CDs and multivalent metal ions. So the fluorescence response of CDs sensors is widely used in heavy metal plasma detection fields. [23] With the purpose to evaluate the fluorescence selectivity of the as-prepared NCDs for detecting metal ions, some common representative metal ions were chose, including Cr3+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Na+, Cu2+, and Cd2+. Fig.6 illustrates the variations of FL intensity detected in the aqueous solution of NCDs when exposed individually to these metal ions and when coexisting with Fe3+ ions. Significant fluorescence quenching has been observed in the NCDs aqueous solution with the existence of Fe3+ ions, as shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S2. While the influence of rest metal ions on the luminescence performance of the NCDs is relatively weak. Furthermore, to rule out the effect of anions, we tested the FL properties of the NCDs after adding Br−, SO3 2−, PO4 3−, HCO3 −, and CO3 2−, respectively. It is found in Fig. S3 that these anions with concentration of 300 μM exert virtually negligible influence on the fluorescence emission. These phenomena demonstrate that the NCDs display a remarkably specific fluorescence response to iron ions. We further investigated the FL behaviors of this carbon dots solution with Fe3+ at continuous concentrations ranging from 0 to103 μM to study its sensitivity of fluorescence response to Fe3+.

Fig. 7 a shows the PL curves of NCDs in different concentration of Fe3+ aqueous solution. As the concentration of Fe3+ increases from 0 to 1000 μM, a progressive reduction in fluorescence intensity at 430 nm is observable, which signifies the high sensitivity of the sensing system to variations in Fe3+ concentration. Based on the Stern-Volmer (S-V) equation, the luminescence intensities (F0, F) of the NCDs solution observed at 430 nm with and without the analyte (Fe3+) should satisfy the linear relationship with concentration (c) of Fe3+ ion: (F0-F)/F0 =Kc, in which K represents the S-V quenching constant [42]. From the data presented in Fig. 7b, it is evident that the data points within the range of 40-500 μM conform to a linear relationship, with a coefficient of determination (R²) of 0.994. The calculated detection limit (LOD) for NCDs stands at 0.22 μM, as determined by the ratio of the triple standard deviation of the blank signal to the slope of the calibrated curve [41]. The relevant test data for the determination of Fe3+ by carbon dots reported in some literatures have been listed in Table 1 . The as-synthesized NCDs demonstrates promise as a probe for detecting Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions, due to their advantages such as rapid response, high sensitivity, and a broad detection range.

To clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism in this system, the time resolved fluorescence decay analysis were conducted for the NCDs solution without and with Fe3+, and the fluorescence decay curves have been displayed in Fig. 8 . The attenuation curves were fitted using following double exponential function (1):
(1)
I t
=
A 1
e
(
−
t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e
(
−
t
τ 2
)

Where A1 and A2 represent the relevant amplitudes; and
τ 1
and τ2 stand for the time constants associated with the attenuation of two radiation channels, respectively. The fitted relevant parameters are given in Tab. S1. Subsequently, the average lifespan value can be obtained by:
(2)
τ
a v e
=
(
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
)
/
(
A 1
+
A 2
)

Based on upon Eqs. (1) and (2), the two average fluorescence lifetimes in solutions of pure NCDs solution and NCDs solution with Fe (III) have been calculated respectively to be 6.92 and 6.89 ns. Approaching the lifetime value indicates almost no electron transfer between NCDs and Fe (III), which excludes the possibility of dynamic quenching mechanisms. The static quenching and inner filter effect become possible quenching mechanisms [49]. As shown in Fig. S4, the absorption spectrum of NCDs solution containing Fe3+ basically matches the sum of the absorption spectra of Fe3+ and NCDs, ruling out the static quenching mechanism [49]. Then, we further measured the UV absorption spectra of different metal ion aqueous solution (Fig. S5). It can be found that except for Fe3+, there is no significant absorption of other metal ions within the range of 320 ∼ 365 nm. These results indicate that the primarily responsibility for the fluorescence attenuation should be the IFE effect [8,50].

The investigation into the influence of pH values and reaction times was carried out to determine the optimal detection conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 9 (a), it is observed that the quenching efficiency remained relatively stable across various pH values ranging from 3 to 11. However, a decline in quenching efficiency is evident with the further increase of the pH value, due to the binding of excessive OH− and Fe3+, which reduces the concentration of Fe3+in the solution, thereby weakening the quenching influence on the fluorescence emission. Subsequently, the investigation delved into identifying the most effective reaction duration. Fig. 9(b) demonstrates that a stable F/F0 ratio was achieved in approximately 14 min. In conclusion, the optimal detection conditions are determined to be a pH of 7 and a reaction time of 14 min.

For the purpose of estimating the practical feasibility of this method for the detection of Fe (Ⅲ) in environmental samples, batch experiments were carried out in the solution with various Fe (Ⅲ) concentrations. Following the standard calibration curve and fitting equation, Fe (Ⅲ) contents in the tap water samples were determined. The data show good agreement between the concentrations found and the added concentrations. As indicated in Table 2 , the recovery of Fe (Ⅲ) ranges from 95 % to 100 %, and the corresponding relative standard deviation (RSD) (n = 3) is below 8 %, demonstrating generally acceptable precision and accuracy. This result illustrates that NCDs can become a candidate for determining Fe (Ⅲ) in a real water environment.

The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was employed to analyze the UV spectra of NCDs, PVA, and NCP composite films. As depicted in Fig. 10 a, that the NCDs exhibits a pronounced absorption band below 250 nm (corresponding to π-π* transition of the C=C/C = N), as well as a relatively weak absorption band in the wavelength rang of 300 ∼ 350 nm (belonging to the n-π* transitions of C = O). As depicted in Fig. 10b, the pure PVA membrane has a weak absorption band in the wavelength range from 200 nm to 400 nm, which indicating the weak UV absorption and visible light transmission properties of PVA film. With the doping of the fluorescent carbon dots, the UV absorption performance of the composite film is significantly improved, while the transmittance in visible region remains almost unchanged. As the content of NCDs adds to 10 mg/mL, the absorbance of the ultraviolet light between 300 nm and 350 nm reaches more than 80 %. However, further increase of the concentration of NCDs cannot continue to improve the UV absorption performance of the composite film. Furthermore, the shape and position of UV absorption peak has been changed after NCDs was added to PVA film. This may origin from the interconnection between the nitrogen-containing groups present on the appearance of NCDs and the -OH groups found in PVA, which affects the surface state of the carbon nanoparticles and changes their energy absorption range [51,52].

To assess its potential for using in LED solid-state lighting devices, we further analyzed the FL performance exhibited by the NCP film. The emission peak of NCP film could be observed at 440 nm under the excitation of 375 nm (Fig. 11 a). The composite film is initially transparent, and becomes slightly light brown after adding carbon dots under natural light. Once exposed to ultraviolet light, the NCP films can emit bright blue light (Fig. 11d). As illustrated in Fig. 11b, as the NCDs content increases, the FL intensity of the composite film initially rises and then declines, reaching its peak at 10 mg/mL. The fluorescence quenching might be attributed to the non-radiative transition of electrons between the excited state energy levels at high NCDs concentration. Notably, unlike NCDs aqueous solution, the NCP films exhibit excitation-independent emission, maintaining a consistent peak around 440 nm (Fig. 11c). Typically, in carbon dots, excitation-dependent PL behavior is associated with differences in particle sizes and the existence of diverse surface states. Here, the observed excitation-dependent FL behavior in NCDs suggests that the primary influence on FL properties comes from the surface states rather than the morphology, given the non-uniform size distribution. In general, the carbon dots with only oxygen-containing groups have several surface states, and the PL behaviors are excitation-dependent. The nitrogen doping introduces a new surface state in NCDs, resulting in a higher luminescence, which is less susceptible to changes in excitation wavelength. In the NCDs solution, these various surface states collaboratively contribute to luminescence, leading to excitation-dependent PL behavior. When NCDs are incorporated into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film, the interaction between the oxygen-containing groups present on the NCDs surface and the hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of PVA becomes significant. This interaction eliminates the surface states corresponding to the oxygen-containing groups, but the surface state generated by nitrogen-doping is less affected. [20,53] As a result, the composite film of NCP demonstrates a consistent emission property irrespective of excitation. These phenomena suggest that the NCP film has good application prospect in the WLED solid-state lighting.

In this work, a green, water-solubility and highly fluorescent NCDs has been successfully prepared. The as-synthesized carbon dots turns out to be a valid fluorescence probe for Fe3+ within a detection range spanning from 40 to 500 μM, boasting a low LOD of 0.22 μM. And the internal filtering effect (IFE) can be the main factor for the sensor to perform sensing function. Moreover, the solid phase film was further prepared by dispersing the NCDs in polyvinyl acetate polymer. The composite film can exhibit bright blue light under 365 nm excitation, providing an alternative blue fluorescent material for LED devices. This research provides a novel fluorescent nanoprobe for detecting iron in water samples, and also offers a way for the efficient and stable application of NCDs in WLEDs.

Bing Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Yixin Luo: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Binqiong Peng: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Lisha Zhang: Formal analysis. Ning Xie: Formal analysis. Dan Yue: Resources, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Weidong Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology. Bowen Qin: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Wenjing Du: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Zhenling Wang: Project administration, Conceptualization. Yilei Zhang: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22105064, 52002117, 52202618); the Key Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province (232102230113, 232102320290); the Innovation Training Program for College Students in Henan Province (202311517010).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.137739.
